refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, visibility, and operator decision support. This aligns with the high safety criticality constraint by ensuring operators have visibility into energy consumption patterns.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_ton_moved over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30-day operational window). Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Collect telemetry from Vehicle power systems and cross-validate with site energy meters. Confirm operator feedback mechanisms are functional and used.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log work output (tons moved, distance, load) from all operational tasks.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton ratio to operators and tele-operators.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated efficiency optimizations (e.g., route planning, load balancing, idle reduction) to reduce energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall transmit energy efficiency telemetry and alerts to Site Manager and Maintenance teams for performance tracking and predictive maintenance.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that aggregates power telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes efficiency metrics in real-time, and broadcasts feedback to operator displays and remote monitoring dashboards.
DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring logic across Vehicle control modules with local efficiency computation; aggregate results via a lightweight message bus to a central dashboard.
DP-C: Implement energy efficiency as a passive logging function in the Vehicle; post-process logs offline to compute energy_per_ton and report results to Site Manager after evaluation period.

DP-D: Integrate energy efficiency monitoring into the Teleoperation system, allowing tele-operators to receive real-time efficiency feedback and issue control commands to optimize energy use remotely.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that aggregates power telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes efficiency metrics in real-time, and broadcasts feedback to operator displays and remote monitoring dashboards.

potential_sources: EMS depends on Vehicle power telemetry interface (Telemetry) – moderate coupling, but well-defined interface.
EMS must integrate with operator display systems (Vehicle HMI) – coupling is acceptable if display interface is standardized.
EMS may interact with Teleoperation system for remote efficiency feedback – coupling is loose if Teleoperation consumes published Telemetry.
EMS may trigger SafetyAlert if efficiency anomalies indicate equipment malfunction – coupling is acceptable and desirable.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits acceptable coupling because dependencies are on well-defined, external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). The EMS acts as a consumer and aggregator, not a modifier of core Vehicle control logic. This preserves independence of Vehicle propulsion and safety systems.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle subsystems expose power telemetry via a standardized Telemetry interface with sufficient granularity (e.g., motor current, fuel consumption, auxiliary loads).
ASSUMPTION: Real-time computation of energy_per_ton is feasible within Vehicle or edge compute resources; latency ≤ 1 second is acceptable for operator feedback.

RISK: If Vehicle telemetry is incomplete or unreliable, EMS will produce incorrect efficiency metrics, leading to false operator feedback and potential safety issues (e.g., operator unaware of energy depletion).
RISK: If EMS is tightly coupled to Vehicle control logic, changes to propulsion algorithms will require EMS redesign, increasing maintenance burden.
RISK: High safety criticality requires that EMS failures do not degrade Vehicle safety systems; fault isolation and graceful degradation must be designed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify that EMS shall operate as a non-critical monitoring layer; Vehicle propulsion and safety systems shall remain independent. Define Telemetry interface contract (data types, update frequency, accuracy) before EMS design. Specify EMS failure modes and their impact on operator feedback and safety alerts.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The energy management system (EMS) for CR-NGHE-003 shall be realized as a system-level capability that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle, distributes feedback to operator displays (Vehicle HMI), and publishes efficiency metrics to Teleoperation and Safety Services for remote monitoring and alerting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: EMS consumes power telemetry from Vehicle subsystems (motor current, fuel consumption, auxiliary loads, operational state). Update frequency: ≥ 10 Hz for real-time feedback. Accuracy: ±5% of nominal values.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: EMS receives task context from Site Manager or Teleoperation (e.g., target load, route, priority). Used to contextualize efficiency metrics and provide task-specific recommendations.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: EMS publishes efficiency-related safety alerts (e.g., energy depletion warning, anomalous power draw indicating equipment malfunction) to Safety Services and operator displays.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: EMS transmits efficiency telemetry and alerts to remote dashboards (Site Manager, Maintenance) and receives configuration updates. Latency: ≤ 5 seconds for non-critical updates.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator and the natural home for cross-cutting energy management. EMS logic is contained within NGHE Solution's system architecture; interfaces to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are well-defined and external. Minimal diffusion: EMS is a cohesive capability, not scattered across modules.

Vehicle could host EMS if energy management is tightly integrated with propulsion control. However, this creates coupling risk: Vehicle becomes responsible for both propulsion safety and efficiency monitoring, violating separation of concerns. EMS would diffuse across Vehicle's power management, control, and HMI subsystems. Not recommended as primary container.

Teleoperation could consume EMS outputs for remote operator feedback, but should not host EMS logic. EMS must function for on-site operators even if Teleoperation is offline. Placing EMS in Teleoperation creates a critical dependency and diffuses energy monitoring across remote and local control paths. Not recommended.

Safety Services could host EMS if energy efficiency is treated as a safety-critical function (e.g., energy depletion as a hazard). However, EMS is primarily a performance/productivity function with secondary safety implications. Placing EMS in Safety Services would conflate efficiency monitoring with safety monitoring, reducing clarity. Acceptable only if energy depletion is classified as a safety hazard requiring Safety Services ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The energy management system (EMS) shall be realized as a system-level module within NGHE Solution, responsible for aggregating Vehicle telemetry, computing efficiency metrics, and distributing feedback to operator displays, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. EMS shall not modify Vehicle control logic or safety systems; it shall operate as a non-critical monitoring and advisory layer.
containment_risks: RISK: If EMS is not clearly separated from Vehicle control logic, future Vehicle updates may inadvertently break EMS or vice versa.
RISK: If EMS is hosted in NGHE Solution but Vehicle telemetry interface is not standardized, EMS will require custom adapters for each Vehicle variant, increasing maintenance burden.

RISK: If EMS failure is not gracefully handled, operator displays may show stale or incorrect efficiency data, potentially misleading operators into unsafe decisions (e.g., continuing operation with depleted energy reserves).
RISK: If EMS is not integrated with Safety Services, efficiency-related hazards (e.g., energy depletion) may not trigger timely safety alerts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define a formal interface contract between NGHE Solution (EMS) and Vehicle (Telemetry provider). Specify EMS failure modes and their impact on operator feedback and safety alerts. Establish a clear ownership boundary: NGHE Solution owns EMS logic; Vehicle owns telemetry generation; Safety Services owns hazard response. Implement health monitoring for EMS to detect and report failures to Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized EMS) directly addresses the refined FR by providing real-time measurement, computation, and feedback mechanisms for energy efficiency. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific algorithms or hardware) and is verifiable through telemetry collection and metric computation. Coupling is acceptable and well-understood.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If EMS logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, maintenance and testing become complex. Mitigation: Centralize EMS in NGHE Solution with clear interfaces to other modules.

DIFFUSION RISK: If EMS depends on multiple Vehicle subsystems (power, control, HMI) without a standardized telemetry interface, EMS will require custom integration for each Vehicle variant. Mitigation: Define a formal Telemetry interface contract before EMS implementation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for EMS because it is the system-level integrator and has visibility into all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). EMS operates as a non-critical monitoring layer, preserving independence of Vehicle propulsion and safety systems. Interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link for remote monitoring).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: measure energy_per_ton over the agreed evaluation period using telemetry from Vehicle and cross-validation with site energy meters. Confirm operator feedback mechanisms are functional and used. Establish acceptance criteria: energy_per_ton ≤ E_target with ±5% measurement accuracy.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution (EMS) is responsible for computing and distributing efficiency metrics. Vehicle is responsible for providing accurate telemetry. Safety Services is responsible for responding to efficiency-related hazards. Site Manager is responsible for setting E_target and evaluating results.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Define the Telemetry interface contract (data types, update frequency, accuracy) before EMS design. Include power consumption (kWh), work output (tons, distance), operational state, and timestamp.

REVISION 2: Specify EMS failure modes and their impact on operator feedback and safety alerts. Define graceful degradation: if EMS fails, operator displays shall show 'data unavailable' rather than stale or incorrect values.
REVISION 3: Establish a formal ownership boundary: NGHE Solution owns EMS logic; Vehicle owns telemetry generation; Safety Services owns hazard response. Document this in the system architecture.
REVISION 4: Define acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton measurement: measurement accuracy ±5%, evaluation period ≥ 30 days, sample rate ≥ 10 Hz for real-time feedback.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs that address measurement, computation, feedback, and optimization. The refined FR identifies five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-003.1 through CR-NGHE-003.5) that must be individually designed and verified. DP-A and IM assignment (NGHE Solution) are sound at the current level, but further decomposition is necessary to allocate design responsibility and verify each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log work output (tons moved, distance, load) from all operational tasks.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton ratio to operators and tele-operators.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated efficiency optimizations (e.g., route planning, load balancing, idle reduction) to reduce energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall transmit energy efficiency telemetry and alerts to Site Manager and Maintenance teams for performance tracking and predictive maintenance.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems expose power telemetry via a standardized Telemetry interface with sufficient granularity (motor current, fuel consumption, auxiliary loads).
Real-time computation of energy_per_ton is feasible within Vehicle or edge compute resources; latency ≤ 1 second is acceptable for operator feedback.
Energy efficiency is a system-level capability that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; no single module owns the entire capability.
Site Manager will define E_target before system deployment; evaluation period is ≥ 30 days.
Operators and tele-operators will use efficiency feedback to make operational decisions; system design assumes human-in-the-loop optimization.
open_risks: RISK: If Vehicle telemetry is incomplete or unreliable, EMS will produce incorrect efficiency metrics, leading to false operator feedback and potential safety issues.
RISK: If EMS is tightly coupled to Vehicle control logic, changes to propulsion algorithms will require EMS redesign, increasing maintenance burden.
RISK: High safety criticality requires that EMS failures do not degrade Vehicle safety systems; fault isolation and graceful degradation must be designed.
RISK: If EMS is not integrated with Safety Services, efficiency-related hazards (e.g., energy depletion) may not trigger timely safety alerts.
RISK: If Telemetry interface is not standardized, EMS will require custom adapters for each Vehicle variant, increasing maintenance burden.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification (data types, update frequency, accuracy) from Vehicle subsystems.
Acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton measurement (accuracy, evaluation period, sample rate).
Definition of E_target from Site Manager or customer.
EMS failure mode analysis and impact on operator feedback and safety alerts.
Verification plan for energy_per_ton measurement (telemetry collection, cross-validation with site energy meters, operator feedback validation).
System architecture diagram showing EMS integration with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
